title: Specific phobia: CBT with exposure
vignette: A 32-year-old man comes to the office due to disabling anxiety. The patient was recently promoted to his company's headquarters, requiring him to work on the 48th floor of the building. As soon as he steps on the elevator, he feels panicky and faint, sweats profusely, and has heart palpitations. The patient is worried that he might have to resign if he is unable to control his anxiety. His fear of elevators dates back to childhood. He drinks 1 or 2 beers on weekends but does not use tobacco or illicit drugs. Which of the following is the most effective long-term treatment for this patient's condition?

clues.text: His fear of elevators dates back to childhood
clues.note: A marked, persistent fear of a specific situation (elevators) since childhood defines a specific phobia. The fear is cued by one stimulus rather than being generalized or spontaneous.

clues.text: He drinks 1 or 2 beers on weekends but does not use tobacco or illicit drugs
clues.note: This substance detail is a distractor to exclude a substance-induced anxiety; mild weekend alcohol use does not explain a lifelong, situation-specific phobia. It's there to be ruled out.

whatMatters: A lifelong, situation-specific fear (elevators) with immediate anxiety on exposure = specific phobia. The question asks for the most effective LONG-TERM treatment, which is cognitive-behavioral therapy with graded EXPOSURE (systematic desensitization) — not a medication that only blunts acute symptoms.
strategy: For phobias/anxiety 'best long-term treatment' questions, choose exposure-based CBT (first-line, durable). Reserve benzodiazepines for rare, brief, anticipated exposures (limited, dependence risk); SSRIs are first-line for generalized/social/panic disorders but not the single best for isolated specific phobia.

eliminations.label: benzodiazepine — short-term only, dependence risk
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: alprazolam is a short-acting benzodiazepine that rapidly relieves acute anxiety. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it's not a LONG-TERM solution (tolerance, dependence, rebound) and treats symptoms, not the phobia; it has a limited role for occasional, time-limited exposure. ✅ Correct if: the patient needed acute relief for a single, unavoidable exposure with no therapist available.

eliminations.label: SSRI — first-line for other anxiety disorders, not isolated phobia (your pick)
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: escitalopram is an SSRI, first-line for generalized anxiety, social anxiety, panic, and OCD. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: for an isolated SPECIFIC phobia, exposure-based CBT is more effective and definitive; medication is adjunctive at best. ✅ Correct if: the diagnosis were social anxiety disorder, GAD, panic disorder, or OCD.

eliminations.label: beta-blocker — performance anxiety, not phobia cure
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: propranolol blunts autonomic symptoms (tremor, palpitations), useful for performance/situational anxiety. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it masks physical symptoms acutely but doesn't extinguish the phobic fear long-term. ✅ Correct if: the scenario were acute performance anxiety (e.g., stage fright) needing symptom control.

eliminations.label: psychodynamic therapy — not first-line for phobia
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: psychodynamic psychotherapy explores unconscious conflicts. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it's not the evidence-based first-line for specific phobia; exposure-based CBT outperforms it. ✅ Correct if: the goal were insight into long-standing relational/personality patterns.

eliminations.label: supportive therapy — insufficient for phobia
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: supportive psychotherapy provides empathy/encouragement. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it doesn't deliver the structured exposure needed to extinguish a phobic response. ✅ Correct if: the patient needed general support during a stressful life event rather than phobia-specific treatment.

eliminations.label: SNRI — same logic as SSRI
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: venlafaxine is an SNRI used for GAD/panic/depression. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: like the SSRI, it's not the most effective treatment for an isolated specific phobia, where exposure CBT is definitive. ✅ Correct if: the diagnosis were GAD or panic disorder.

reasoning: Lifelong fear cued by a specific situation (elevators) = specific phobia
Most effective LONG-TERM treatment = exposure-based CBT
Medications (benzodiazepine/SSRI/beta-blocker) only blunt symptoms or fit other disorders
mechanism: Specific phobia is a marked, persistent fear of a particular object/situation that provokes immediate anxiety on exposure and is avoided. Cognitive-behavioral therapy with graded exposure (systematic desensitization) repeatedly and safely confronts the feared stimulus so anxiety habituates and the conditioned fear extinguishes — producing durable improvement. Benzodiazepines have only a limited, short-term role; SSRIs are first-line for other anxiety disorders.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Psych > anxiety disorders: specific phobia (definition, >6 months, exposure-based CBT first-line) vs panic disorder/GAD/social anxiety (SSRIs first-line). Note benzodiazepine's limited role and dependence risk.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Read 'most effective LONG-TERM' as 'definitive, durable' → exposure CBT. Separate symptom-blunting drugs (benzo, beta-blocker) from the treatment that extinguishes the fear.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on specific phobia, exposure therapy/systematic desensitization, CBT, benzodiazepine, SSRI vs SNRI? Tell me which and I'll explain.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Escitalopram tempts because SSRIs are 'the anxiety drug,' but they're first-line for OTHER anxiety disorders, not isolated specific phobia. Match phobia → exposure CBT.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: If you reflexively chose a drug, make a rule card: specific phobia = exposure CBT; SSRIs are for GAD/social/panic/OCD.

keywords.front: Most effective long-term tx for specific phobia

keywords.back: Marked fear of a specific object/situation, >6 months, immediate anxiety, avoidance
Limited, short-term (single unavoidable exposure); dependence risk

firstAid.detail: Psychiatry > anxiety disorders: specific phobia diagnosis and exposure-based CBT/systematic desensitization as first-line.
firstAid.location: Psychiatry > anxiety disorders: specific phobia diagnosis and exposure-based CBT/systematic desensitization as first-line.

firstAid.detail: Psychiatry/Pharm > SSRIs/SNRIs (first-line for GAD/panic/social/OCD), benzodiazepines (short-term), beta-blockers (performance anxiety).
firstAid.location: Psychiatry/Pharm > SSRIs/SNRIs (first-line for GAD/panic/social/OCD), benzodiazepines (short-term), beta-blockers (performance anxiety).

firstAid.detail: Psychiatry > CBT vs psychodynamic vs supportive therapy — indications and evidence base.
firstAid.location: Psychiatry > CBT vs psychodynamic vs supportive therapy — indications and evidence base.

memoryHook: Phobia? FACE it — exposure-based CBT extinguishes the fear; pills only paper over the panic.
